Reference class: short-horizon directional moves in a liquid US investment-grade corporate bond yield index. Offsetting that, the exact 2025-11-09 comparison value is unpublished at cutoff and could differ meaningfully from 4.670, which pushes the forecast back toward 0.5, especially at longer horizons. So I land on a modest-above-even chance of an increase, with uncertainty increasing over time.

The prompt says the latest known value on 2025-10-30 was 2.000, so a higher rate by 2025-11-16 would require a very fast hawkish turn and is near the floor. By 2025-12-09 the probability is only slightly higher for the same reason. By 2026-02-07 there is enough time for scheduled meetings and a possible inflation reacceleration/hawkish repricing, so I raise the probability, but it remains clearly below 50% because a move from 2.00 to higher soon after reaching 2.00 would usually require a meaningful upside inflation surprise or financial-stability concern.

For 2025-11-16 and 2025-12-09, the main path to a higher print is minor technical variation rather than a policy hike, so probabilities stay below one-third. By 2026-02-07 there is more time and at least one or two FOMC opportunities, which raises uncertainty and the chance of an increase somewhat, but the late-2025 level still looks more consistent with hold/cut than with renewed hikes, so I remain below 50%.

Reference class: week-to-week changes in the Fed’s primary credit (discount window) outstanding balance when the current level is already elevated. My base rate is below 50% for an increase from here because primary credit loans are very short-dated (overnight to 28 days), so elevated balances often mean-revert unless funding stress is persisting or worsening. The known pre-cutoff level ($7.349B on 2025-10-30) is high enough that runoff is a meaningful force. That said, once discount-window use is elevated, episodes can persist for several weeks, and longer horizons allow more chance of renewed bank/funding stress or seasonal year-end pressure, so I move the probabilities gradually back toward 0.5 with time.

My best estimate is 32%. Outside view: broad BIS semiconductor/AI export-control packages are infrequent, and getting another qualifying global package in the same year as the big January 2025 rule is harder than even odds. Inside view: there was strong bipartisan pressure to tighten AI controls further, and the administration had signaled interest in replacing/reworking the January framework, so a late-2025 rule was quite plausible. But as of the 2025-11-09 cutoff, I am not aware of a clearly qualifying broad/global rule already in force; the withdrawal/rewrite of the January diffusion framework, the controversy around cloud and model-weight controls, and the short time remaining to year-end all pull the probability below 50%.

My estimate is 11%. As of the 2025-11-09 cutoff, the key fact pattern is that no qualifying federal AI law had yet been enacted, while the measures that had advanced were described as targeted and likely non-qualifying under the resolution criteria. With less than two months remaining until 2026, Congress would most likely need to attach a narrow but qualifying AI-regulatory provision to a must-pass vehicle or rapidly clear a late-stage bipartisan bill. That is possible, but historically uncommon for new substantive regulation of an emerging technology area, especially one with unsettled scope and significant industry/political disagreement. So NO is the clear favorite, though not overwhelmingly certain because year-end packages can sometimes move quickly.

I estimate a 9% chance. The outside view is unfavorable: Western-led ad hoc coalitions almost never deploy forces into an active interstate war involving Russia absent a ceasefire, great-power buy-in, or a formal NATO/UN framework. The inside view keeps this above floor, because this coalition was actually created, Ukraine would welcome it, operational planning was reportedly advanced, and the resolution criteria allow narrower paths to YES such as coalition air or naval activity, not only a large ground force. Still, the strongest specific reporting in the prompt says momentum was shifting away from direct troop deployment because of political and logistical constraints and expected opposition from both Russia and the United States. With only weeks remaining after the 2025-11-09 cutoff, a coalition-branded deployment by 31 Dec 2025 looks unlikely, though not impossible if a late ceasefire or a limited reassurance mission materializes.

On the literal resolution criteria, this looks very likely to already be satisfied before the 2025-11-09 forecast date: European countries publicly committed M270-family systems to Ukraine years earlier, and M270 launchers are explicitly listed as qualifying HIMARS-compatible systems. The UK announced M270 MLRS transfers in 2022; Germany announced MARS II/M270-family transfers in 2022; France also committed LRU/M270-family launchers. If those prior commitments count—as the wording suggests—then the question is effectively already a YES. I keep a modest gap below certainty because there is some residual ambiguity that the market author may have intended only new commitments made during 2025, even though that limitation is not stated in the provided criteria.

My base rate is the post-2022 reference class of quarterly EU imports of Russian gas. In that class, 15 bcm is a high bar: achievable in earlier war-period quarters, but much harder once Europe had already reduced dependence. The key structural fact for 2025 is that Ukraine transit ended in January, leaving mainly TurkStream pipeline gas plus Russian LNG. That sharply lowers the plausible quarterly ceiling. Even with continued LNG purchases and some seasonal winter strength, getting to 15 bcm in a single quarter would likely require a very large surge relative to the remaining route capacity. The main upside case is an unusually strong Q4 2025 LNG spike, especially since the Commission delayed restricting Russian LNG, but that still looks materially less likely than not. So I put this in the low-teens.

Base-rate/structural forecast only, because no search tools are available here. Iran has repeatedly threatened the Strait of Hormuz but has historically avoided a sustained closure, largely because doing so would also damage Iran’s own exports, alienate key buyers like China, and risk a major U.S./allied military response. That keeps the probability well below even odds. The main reason the probability is not lower is that the market’s resolution criteria are relatively broad: a temporary but serious Iranian interdiction that materially impedes oil shipments, even if some traffic still passes or only Western shipping is targeted, could resolve YES. In a high-escalation regional war scenario, that is plausible. Netting those factors, I put the chance at 14%.

Initial/reference-class view: this is a founder-CEO turnover question for a founder-controlled mega-cap public company. For entrenched founder CEOs, annual departure rates are usually low single digits; Musk-specific 2025 pressures likely push that above the pure base rate, but the short remaining window from 2025-11-09 to 2025-12-31 pulls it back down. My estimate is that, as of the cutoff, a departure in the final ~7 weeks of 2025 was unlikely but not negligible. The strongest arguments for YES are unusual 2025 succession chatter, Musk’s many outside commitments, and ongoing Tesla governance/performance pressure. The strongest arguments for NO are Musk’s exceptional control over Tesla, a historically loyal board, the lack of an obvious successor with equal investor legitimacy, and the fact that any orderly transition would more naturally be timed for 2026 rather than the last weeks of 2025. Net: about a 9% chance.

Initial/final estimate: 35%. Reference class: intra-team F1 season points battles, especially a veteran champion joining a top team alongside an established elite incumbent. My base rate starts below 50% because the incumbent in his prime usually has a meaningful edge over a newcomer learning team processes, car characteristics, and operating culture. I then adjust for driver quality: Hamilton is still an all-time great and absolutely capable of winning the head-to-head, but Leclerc being younger, already embedded at Ferrari, and likely closer to peak performance makes him the favorite. Because no search tools are available here, I cannot condition on the actual 2025 standings as of 2025-11-09, so I use the best outside-view prior for the event itself. That yields roughly a 35% chance Hamilton finishes the 2025 season with more points than Leclerc.

My estimate is 7%. The outside view is that despite decades of severe U.S.-Iran crises, the pair have repeatedly stopped short of an openly acknowledged sustained war; that makes the base rate for a true YES under this market’s fairly high bar very low. I do adjust upward because the prompt’s June 2025 clarifications imply there was at least one real military flashpoint serious enough to create ambiguity. But by the 2025-11-09 cutoff, the question was evidently still unresolved, which means that earlier escalation had not become the sustained, publicly recognized conflict required here. With only about seven weeks left in the year, and with the creator explicitly saying single strikes, limited targeted rounds, or an assassination would not be enough on their own, the path to YES is narrow: it likely requires a renewed major escalation, sustained operations, and clear media/government consensus. That is possible, but still unlikely before year-end.

Initial belief state: Reference class = US calendar-year recession events, operationalized as two consecutive quarters of negative real GDP growth using initial BEA estimates. A reasonable outside-view base rate is low-to-mid teens in any given year, modestly elevated here because rates were restrictive and recession risk had been above normal entering 2025. Key uncertainties that would matter most are the actual initial GDP prints for 2025 Q1-Q3, whether any pair was already negative by 2025-11-09, and contemporaneous labor-market / nowcast evidence for Q4. With no search tools available to retrieve those late-2025 specifics, I lean on the outside view plus the pre-2025 soft-landing backdrop: recession is plausible but still clearly below 50%.

Base rate for an NBER recession start over a random 2-year U.S. window is roughly 25-30%, but the posterior by 2025-11-09 is much lower. My key update is that the feared 2023 recession never materialized and the 2023-24 economy appears to have maintained enough growth in output, jobs, income, and spending that an NBER call for a 2023/2024 peak looks unlikely. Conditional on no clear NBER declaration by the cutoff and only ~7 weeks left before the end-2025 deadline, the remaining path to YES is narrow: either a late-2025 declaration of a subtle 2024 recession or a retrospective call driven by revisions. That seems possible but unlikely.

Outside view: this is a first-crossing of a major athletics barrier at the extreme frontier. A legal sub-2 was clearly physiologically plausible by 2019, and Kiptum’s 2:00:35 ratified WR showed the gap had shrunk to only 36 seconds. But that is still a very large improvement at this level, the standout candidate (Kiptum) died in 2024, Kipchoge was aging, and no other known marathoner had yet shown equally convincing sub-2 potential. Over a horizon ending in 2025, that makes YES possible but still unlikely. Because I do not have a search tool here to inspect actual 2025 results up to the 2025-11-09 cutoff, this is a low-confidence outside-view estimate rather than a fully informed cutoff-date forecast.

Initial reference class: extreme S&P 500 daily close-to-close drawdowns (>5%). Unconditionally these are very rare, but they cluster in crisis years. Since a >5% drop already occurred on Apr. 4, 2025, 2025 is clearly not a normal low-volatility year, which raises the odds of another such day. Still, by the Nov. 9 forecast date only about seven weeks remained in the year, and absent direct evidence that a second >5% down day had already occurred, the residual chance before Dec. 31 looks only in the high single digits. I therefore put the probability at 10%.

Reference class: newly introduced, very hard capability benchmarks where frontier AI starts near zero and must exceed a very high bar within about a year. On ordinary public benchmarks this sometimes happens fast, but on private, expert-created, contamination-resistant benchmarks it is much rarer. FrontierMath began with frontier models below 2% even with tools and extended thinking, and >80% would require an extraordinary jump. Rapid reasoning-model progress keeps this from being negligible, but absent direct evidence of late-2025 scores near the threshold, I put the chance at about 9%.

Base rate for a single NFL team to win the Super Bowl is about 1 in 32, or 3.1%. The 49ers deserve to be above that because they have been a consistently strong organization in the Shanahan era, with high-end coaching, a strong roster core, and repeated deep playoff runs. But even elite NFL teams rarely get above the low-teens in true title odds because of injuries, single-elimination variance, and strong competition. Without current-season specifics available here for the 2025 team as of 2025-11-09, I land on a modestly-above-average but still clearly sub-10% estimate.

Base-rate view: for a large-cap asset like Bitcoin to set a new all-time high roughly 2.5x above the ~2024-halving-era range within a single calendar year is plausible but far from routine. The reference class is ‘BTC hitting a specified upside price barrier within a fixed one-year window.’ Historically, Bitcoin has produced multiple >2x annual moves in bull phases, but those bursts are irregular and usually depend on a favorable macro/risk backdrop plus strong narrative/flow momentum. A $150K print in 2025 would likely require BTC to be already in a strong post-halving bull regime, with ETF/treasury/corporate demand or similar sustained flows overwhelming any macro headwinds. Against that, $150K is a psychologically and numerically high threshold, and late-cycle volatility can cut both ways. Lacking direct access here to contemporaneous Nov. 9, 2025 price-path information, I lean on the outside view and assign a moderate but sub-50% chance.

Base rate for complex, high-profile sports-media control transactions without a publicly completed binding deal is below 50%, because many strategic talks stall on valuation, governance, and timing. Here there is a real strategic fit—ESPN/Disney would benefit from NFL Network/RedZone and the NFL could value an ESPN partnership—but 'under Disney's control' is a high bar, and any transaction would be complicated by ESPN's broader strategic reshaping, league governance concerns, and the NFL's ability to delay rather than sell control. Net: modestly more likely NO than YES.

Reference class: a specific contemporary tech/company brand becoming prominent enough in culture to be featured in an SNL sketch within a single calendar year. The outside-view base rate for any given brand/company is low: SNL does many sketches, but only a small fraction center on a particular non-political company, and an even smaller fraction on a still-somewhat-niche tech brand like Waymo. Waymo did have clear pathways to relevance by 2025—robotaxi expansion, recurring social-media discourse about autonomous cars, and comedic potential—but it would still need to break through from 'newsworthy tech story' to 'recognizable enough to support a sketch.' The strongest uncertainty is whether by late 2025 Waymo had a major viral incident, rapid market expansion, or celebrity/NYC-adjacent moment that made it salient to SNL writers. Absent specific evidence that such a breakout happened, I stay below 20%.

Reference class: short-horizon forecasts about whether a long-dominant consumer software platform remains the leader in global market-share rankings. Base rate is very high: products with a decades-long lead and a very large current gap almost always remain #1 over a 1–2 month horizon unless there is a severe discontinuity. As of the 2025-11-09 cutoff, Chrome had been the clear global browser-share leader for many years, with a very large cushion over Safari/Edge/others; a reversal by end-2025 would require either an extraordinary market shock, an abrupt methodology change in the cited Wikipedia table, or a massive one-off collapse in Chrome usage. Those are possible but unlikely. The main uncertainty is not normal competitive drift but edge cases around the resolution source or an unusual late-2025 disruption. Given Chrome’s entrenched position and the short remaining time to resolution, I put this near the top of the allowed range.

Base rate for celebrity/billionaire-launched US third parties is low, and with only about seven weeks left after the 2025-11-09 forecast date, I think NO is still more likely. The main upward adjustments are that Musk has already publicly expressed intent, has the resources to stand up a minimal party quickly, and the market’s criteria are relatively permissive (no ballot access or candidates required, and self-identification plus tangible infrastructure/media consensus may be enough). The main downward adjustments are that a tweet alone does not count, there is no clear evidence in the prompt of a fully tangible party already existing by the cutoff, and Musk often makes ambitious political statements without sustained organizational follow-through. Net: meaningful chance, but still below 50%.

Reference class: high-profile Trump relationships with powerful allies/advisers that begin warmly but show public strain before a later checkpoint. The outside-view base rate for such relationships still being clearly 'on good terms' later is below 50%, because Trump often falls out with prominent associates, especially independent personalities who compete for attention. I’d anchor around 35% for a generic case.

Conditioning on the prompt’s specific evidence pushes me lower: they reportedly started 2025 close, but by the forecast date there is backlash from Republicans and they are already fighting on social media. Public, ego-driven feuds between Trump and another highly status-conscious figure are often sticky, and there are only about 7 weeks left in the year for a visible reconciliation strong enough to make an end-of-year news-based judgment come out YES. 

The main reason not to go even lower is that both men are highly transactional. They may still share political incentives, and either could decide that a public thaw is useful. Trump has reconciled with former critics before, and Musk has strong reasons to stay aligned with political power when beneficial.

Overall, I think a reconciliation by year-end is possible but less likely than not, and the subjective resolution standard also means ambiguity tends to favor NO unless the relationship is visibly repaired.

My base rate is low: U.S. politicians almost never successfully pursue legal action over obvious TV satire, and major media companies are hard targets because First Amendment protections are strong. I then adjust upward because Trump is unusually retaliatory toward critics and the market counts relatively mild steps like cease-and-desist letters or explicitly retaliatory policy actions, not just lawsuits. Still, the resolution requires more than anger: there must be a formal action specifically tied to South Park’s portrayal and it must produce some negative repercussion. Given the prompt’s evidence of public outrage but no actual legal escalation, I put this at a low but non-trivial 9%.

Base rate first: outright regime change in a durable, centralized authoritarian system within a single year is uncommon, and Iran’s system has repeatedly survived severe unrest, sanctions, and elite pressure. The resolution threshold is also demanding: it is not enough for there to be protests, repression, leadership illness, or even a succession inside the system; the regime must actually lose control of the central government in Tehran or be replaced. Factors that lift the risk above a pure baseline are Iran’s chronic economic strain, legitimacy problems, possible succession stress around the Supreme Leader, and the possibility that regional war or a coup attempt could create a sudden break. But absent strong contemporaneous evidence of security-force fragmentation or elite defections, those are still tail risks rather than the modal outcome. On balance, I put the chance of regime change in 2025 at 8%.

Base rate for a two-year US recession/GDP-negative-pair window is material, but by 2025-11-09 most relevant quarters are already known. My best understanding is that 2024 did not produce a qualifying pair, and 2025 had at most one negative quarter rather than two in a row. That leaves only narrow paths to YES: chiefly a negative Q3 2025 (or a revision flipping it negative) combined with a negative Q4 2025, plus a small amount of criteria ambiguity. Given how uncommon large GDP revisions are and how late we are in the window, I put YES at 7%.

My estimate is 12%. The reference class is shuttered but still-recognizable media/data brands that might be sold, spun off, or lightly revived. Outside view, some such brands do come back, but most do not, especially on a short timeline. Inside view, 538 has meaningful residual brand/IP value and the resolution criteria are fairly generous, which keeps YES live. But as of 2025-11-09 the brand is inactive, there are under two months left in the year, internal ABC/Disney revival looks unlikely after a shutdown, and any sale/relaunch would need to happen very quickly during a period when media deals and product relaunches are often slow. That makes YES possible but clearly unlikely.

I put this at 15%. The outside view for a small platform moving from play-money into legal real-money/crypto betting on a short timeline is low, because legal, compliance, payments, and geofencing hurdles are substantial. The main pro-YES evidence in the prompt is that CEO Stevo had discussed exploring crypto again via a separate market set, and the resolution criteria are fairly broad: any one legal jurisdiction and even an affiliated platform would count. But as of the 2025-11-09 cutoff, the question is still unresolved and only about seven weeks remain in the year, so absent stronger evidence of an imminent launch, NO is much more likely than YES.

I estimate a 10% chance. The outside view is that a >250k drop in federal payroll employment within a single year is very rare. Trump/DOGE clearly pushed for unusually large workforce reductions, and there was a pipeline of agency cuts, buyouts, and attrition that could still hit late-2025 data. But as of 2025-11-09, the realized decline appeared well short of 250k, many planned cuts were bogged down in litigation/procedure, and only about two payroll-reference months remained for those reductions to show up in the FRED series. That makes YES possible but unlikely.

My estimate is 13%. Outside view: high-profile figures publicly floating a new U.S. political party usually do not complete formal creation within months. Inside view: Musk is unusually capable of doing it because he has money, media attention, and the resolution bar is only a public announcement plus FEC paperwork, not full ballot access. But by the 2025-11-09 cutoff, only about seven weeks remain, and if a filing had not already happened, the long delay since his June comments would be a meaningful negative signal. Musk also has a history of using political threats rhetorically and then shifting focus. So this looks more possible than a normal third-party launch, but still clearly unlikely by year-end.

With no search tools available, I’m relying on outside-view/base-rate reasoning from similar high-profile art/jewel robberies. The relevant reference class is major, sophisticated museum/jewel heists in developed countries, asking whether at least one direct physical perpetrator is taken into custody within roughly 2 months of the crime. My anchor is around 25–35%: these cases often attract intense investigative resources, but many still take months or years to crack, and the market requires a direct robber rather than a mere accomplice. The unusually high profile of a Louvre/Crown Jewels case pushes upward, while the short remaining window to Dec. 24 and the sophistication implied by such a robbery push downward. Net: about 31%.

Reference class: $100M+ theatrical films opening in the US that are publicly reported as having AI write or materially draft the script. My base rate is extremely low: despite rapid AI adoption elsewhere in filmmaking since 2023, I do not know of a clearly qualifying blockbuster released through 2025-11-09 that was openly described in news coverage as AI-written or significantly AI-script-assisted. With less than two months left in 2025, the remaining candidate slate is small, and major studios still face strong reputational and labor incentives not to use or at least not to publicly acknowledge meaningful AI scriptwriting on tentpoles. The main residual path to YES is that a writer/director or reporter reveals substantial AI assistance on an already released or late-2025 blockbuster, or that a foreign blockbuster opening in the US is more open about such use. That tail risk keeps this above zero, but only barely, so I submit the floor-allowed 5%.

My best estimate is 28%. The key distinction is that this is not asking whether frontier AI will look very impressive by end-2025; it asks whether a poll majority will say AGI "as smart as a generally educated human" already exists. Rapid capability gains make that plausible, especially because this bar is softer than stronger AGI definitions, but short-horizon AGI timelines have often been too optimistic and subjective polls usually apply a conservative standard around robustness, autonomy, and reliability. So I see a meaningful chance of YES, but NO remains more likely.

Virgin Galactic looks financially distressed, but a bankruptcy filing before 2026 still seems less likely than not. The main reason is timing: as of the 2025-11-09 forecast date, the company would only need to avoid a Chapter 11 petition for about seven more weeks. Even with weak fundamentals, its historical cash cushion, ability to dilute public shareholders, and lack of an obvious near-term debt wall make survival to 2026 more likely than an immediate filing. I still assign a meaningful tail risk because the business has very low revenue and persistent heavy cash burn, so if financing dried up or a going-concern crisis emerged, a filing could come quickly.

As of the 2025-11-09 cutoff, this looks unlikely. China’s pattern has been very slow and cautious on overt overseas basing: Djibouti remained the only clearly acknowledged foreign PLA base for years, while other reported sites were mostly rumors, dual-use port projects, or access arrangements rather than publicly announced operating military bases. BRICS expansion did add some plausible hosts such as the UAE, Iran, Egypt, and Ethiopia, but each has major obstacles: Gulf states have strong incentives to avoid an overt Chinese base because of U.S. ties; Iran is more geopolitically aligned but is also sovereignty-sensitive and had no clearly public operating Chinese base by the cutoff; Ethiopia is unstable and landlocked; the original BRICS members are mostly poor fits. With less than two months left before 2026 and no clear public confirmation in hand by the cutoff, only a small residual chance remains that either China or the U.S. intelligence community publicly announces an already-operating Chinese base in a BRICS state before year-end.

My estimate is 6%. The relevant reference class is appeals of CAS arbitral awards to the Swiss Federal Tribunal or equivalent higher review. The outside view is extremely unfavorable: CAS’s own bulletin says only about 10% of awards are appealed and only about 1% of appeals historically succeed, with successful appeals amounting to about 0.3% of all awards. This specific case is somewhat stronger than the generic base rate because USA Gymnastics claims to have video evidence and the case has unusually intense scrutiny and institutional backing. But the main path to reversal still appears very narrow: CNN reported CAS itself cannot reopen the matter, so the new evidence may not help unless a higher court finds a qualifying procedural/legal defect rather than merely a factual error. On top of that, the question requires an overturn before Jan. 1, 2026, and as of the Nov. 9, 2025 cutoff there was very little time left. So I end up only modestly above the already tiny base rate, but still firmly in low-probability territory.

I put this at 10%. The outside view is very unfavorable: as of the background provided, CDC’s published IRAT emergence scores had never reached the 'high' threshold of 8.0, and the latest H5 score cited was only 5.79. From a 2025-11-09 forecast date, less than two months remain, so this would require either an already-issued but not widely noted CDC update or a very rapid late-2025 combination of alarming new H5 evidence and formal IRAT publication. H5N1’s unusual mammalian spread and repeated human spillovers make it the likeliest subtype to break precedent, but absent clear sustained human-to-human transmission, a jump all the way to >=8 before Jan. 1 still looks unlikely.

Reference class: the yearly worldwide box-office champion. In the modern blockbuster era, that slot is usually taken by a franchise/IP film, and the prompt itself notes that 3 of the last 4 yearly winners were franchise movies. For 2025, the top-end slate is especially franchise-heavy: the most obvious mega-gross contenders named in the prompt are all sequels or entries in established series/universes, and even the current leader is already a franchise title. Since ties also resolve YES, the path to a YES outcome is broader than the path to NO. The main remaining risk is a breakout non-franchise hit or an edge-case classification dispute, but overall YES looks substantially more likely than not.

Referencia de clase: lanzamientos de videojuegos AAA ya anunciados oficialmente para una ventana concreta del mismo año, especialmente de grandes publishers con distribución global. Base rate: una fracción significativa de estos títulos sí sale dentro de su ventana anunciada, pero los juegos más ambiciosos también sufren retrasos frecuentes; para proyectos blockbuster, una tasa de cumplimiento de ~60–70% para llegar al año prometido me parece una ancla razonable. Aquí hay factores a favor por encima de esa base: Rockstar/Take-Two habían comunicado oficialmente una salida en otoño de 2025 y GTA es una franquicia con lanzamientos globales estándar, incluyendo Europa. Además, la resolución exige venta en Europa, algo que normalmente acompaña el lanzamiento global y no es un cuello de botella específico. El principal riesgo es un retraso del juego completo fuera de 2025, algo plausible dada la escala del proyecto y el historial de retrasos de títulos AAA/Rockstar. Dado que el anuncio oficial ya acotaba el lanzamiento a otoño de 2025, pero sin fecha firme en el material proporcionado, estimo una probabilidad moderadamente alta, no extrema.

Initial belief state: Reference class = year-over-year real GDP growth comparisons between Spain and the Eurozone over a one-year horizon. Outside view, Spain has often but not always outgrown the Eurozone in the post-2014 era, so a reasonable base-rate anchor is roughly 0.65-0.70. I then update upward because by the cutoff this question is very near resolution, Spain had already been materially outperforming the Eurozone as of Q3 2024, and the broad 2025 macro consensus before the cutoff still had Spain growing much faster than the Eurozone. Given how large that expected gap was, normal GDP revisions are unlikely to flip the sign. I leave some residual uncertainty for data revisions and base effects, so I stop short of certainty.

Initial outside view: specific, technical amendments to a named federal tax credit usually do not pass in a given year unless they are part of a larger must-pass or reconciliation package. A Republican trifecta in 2025 materially raises the chance of some IRA changes, but this question asks about a narrower outcome than repeal or phaseout: Congress must specifically make the existing domestic-content bonus requirements mandatory for 45Y eligibility. That is politically plausible as an 'America First' compromise, but still looks less likely than the main alternatives of repeal, phaseout, partial trimming, or no enacted change. The slim House margin and internal GOP split between full repeal and a softer phaseout also cut against a precise consensus outcome. Net: below even odds, but not remote.

I put this at 24%. Outside view: cert is rarely granted, but this is not an ordinary petition. It presents a recurring federal preemption question under FIFRA, Monsanto claims a real 3d-vs-9th/11th circuit split, and the stakes are very large given the Roundup docket. Those features push the case well above the ordinary cert base rate. Still, the Court already declined a similar Monsanto petition in 2022, this is a state-court vehicle rather than the cleanest possible federal appellate case, and the Court could delay by seeking the Solicitor General’s views or simply prefer more percolation. Given those cross-currents, a grant before Jan. 15, 2026 looks materially possible but still clearly less likely than not.

I assign a 9% chance. The outside view dominates: full-scale nuclear explosive tests by established nuclear powers after long moratoria are extraordinarily rare, and Russia has not done one since 1990. The prompt does provide real upward pressure—claims that Novaya Zemlya is ready and that some Russian hawks have inspected the site—but that is still much weaker than an actual political decision to absorb the diplomatic, strategic, and escalatory costs of breaking the testing taboo. Also, because the forecast date is 2025-11-09, most of the resolution window has already elapsed; absent evidence that a test had already happened by then, the remaining time is short. My main limitation is that no search tool is available here, so I cannot verify whether a test had already occurred or whether late-2025 indicators sharply changed the picture.

My estimate is 21%. Outside view: Bitcoin is volatile enough that 40–70% moves in 1–2 months are possible, especially in a post-halving bull-cycle year, but they are still distinctly minority outcomes. From a likely early-November-2025 price region somewhere well below $170k, the remaining window to Dec. 31 is short, so reaching $170k would probably require a late-cycle, near-parabolic move. Factors pushing up the odds are BTC’s historical blow-off-top behavior, strong structural demand drivers in this cycle, and the fact that a brief 1-minute wick on Binance is sufficient. Factors pushing down the odds are the large remaining distance to the barrier, the limited time left, and the possibility that the main cycle peak either comes later or has already passed. Net: about a one-in-five chance.

Reference class: armed non-state/insurgent organizations making an official public disarmament commitment while still in an active national conflict. The base rate for such announcements over a short horizon is very low. Hamas in particular has historically treated its weapons as core to its identity and leverage, and the market requires a formal, credible announcement by recognized leadership—not leaks, conditional trial balloons, or vague openness. By the 2025-11-09 cutoff, the remaining window to 2025-12-31 is short, so absent a clearly emerging comprehensive postwar deal, the chance of a qualifying announcement is small. I keep some probability because severe battlefield degradation, Arab/international pressure, and reconstruction/governance incentives could still produce a last-minute staged or partial disarmament commitment that would count under these rules.

Initial reference class: calendar years in which the Fed delivers very large easing cycles, specifically 200 bps or more of cuts (8 quarter-point equivalents) by the December meeting. Outside view, that is rare and usually associated with recession or financial crisis. A reasonable base-rate anchor is low single digits to high single digits. The structure of this market makes it even harder by the 2025-11-09 forecast date: with only the December meeting left, the event requires that most of the 200 bps already be in place by late October, or else that the Fed deliver an unusually large final-meeting cut. Without strong evidence of a severe downturn, that is unlikely. So I put this near the floor at 5%.

A 50+ bp cut at a single scheduled FOMC meeting is unusual and typically requires either a clear recession or acute financial stress. The outside view over regular FOMC meetings suggests only a low-single-digit base rate for such moves, but the restrictive-rate backdrop after a major hiking cycle makes an oversized cut somewhat more plausible than pure historical average. Without specific late-2025 macro evidence, I land at 9%.

I estimate Pierre-Yves Bournazel as a clear longshot, but not impossible. My outside-view prior for a secondary contender in an open-seat, two-round major-city mayoral race a few months before voting is roughly 5–10%. In Paris specifically, the field is usually shaped by stronger partisan machines and alliance dynamics, and Bournazel has generally had lower national/profile visibility than the most obvious Paris contenders. His path would likely require both becoming the principal center/center-right standard-bearer and then prevailing in a runoff/alliance structure in a city where the left has been structurally strong. That said, open-seat races can reshuffle quickly, and his moderate image gives him some coalition upside, so I keep him above the floor. Net: about 8%.

I lean YES: Trump is slightly more likely than not to go through all of 2025 without formally announcing his next Fed Chair pick. The outside view is that Fed chair selections are usually announced only a few months before the new term begins; for a May 2026 turnover, early 2026 is still a normal window. Trump’s unusual hostility toward Powell, plus Bessent’s public “shadow chair” idea, clearly raise the odds of an earlier-than-normal 2025 announcement. But institutional and market-stability reasons still argue for waiting, and by Nov. 9 there would be only a short window left in the year. Net result: modest edge to no announcement by Dec. 31, 2025.

Outside-view only, since no search tools are available here. An 18-straight-week run at #1 on the Billboard 200 is an extreme tail outcome even for major superstar albums, and this market also requires the album to be released and debut at #1. Without concrete pre-cutoff evidence that the album had already built a very long uninterrupted #1 streak by 2025-11-09, the YES case remains a low-probability scenario. I leave a little room for the possibility that it was already on a dominant run, but the base rate for 18 straight weeks is low enough that I still land in the mid-single digits.

Base-rate view: at any single scheduled FOMC meeting, a hold is usually more likely than a move. From a 2025-11-09 vantage, March 2026 is only about four months away, so absent a clear recession or renewed inflation surge, the Fed is more likely to wait than to change rates at that specific meeting. The main reason this is not higher is that early 2026 could still plausibly be part of a gradual easing cycle from restrictive levels, making a cut a real possibility. I see hikes as materially less likely than cuts, so the balance still favors no change.

Initial/reference-class forecast: formal NATO Article 5 invocation is extraordinarily rare historically (only once, after 9/11), and NATO has repeatedly shown a strong preference to avoid crossing that formal threshold unless there is a clear, major attack. That keeps the base rate very low. I raise it above the raw historical rate because the 2025 European security environment plausibly includes elevated spillover risk from Russia-Ukraine, hybrid/cyber escalation, or a major terrorist attack affecting a member state. Net: still much more likely NO than YES, but not negligible.

Initial outside-view forecast: explicit AGI declarations by major labs are extremely rare, and this question is about a public announcement, not just capability. OpenAI is one of the few organizations with both the branding and frontier progress to plausibly make such a claim, which keeps the probability above a trivial level. But conditioning on a Nov. 9, 2025 forecast date leaves less than two months for the announcement, and there are strong reasons a company might avoid the literal term “AGI” even after a major breakthrough. Net: low, but not negligible.

Reference class: WHO-declared pandemics caused by newly emerging respiratory viruses, especially novel coronaviruses. Base rates are extremely low on a sub-1-year horizon: WHO has declared very few pandemics in modern history, and only one coronavirus (SARS-CoV-2) has reached that level. Discovery of a bat coronavirus with possible human infectivity is weak evidence by itself, because many such findings never lead to sustained human transmission, much less a WHO pandemic declaration within the same year. Also, even large outbreaks are not always labeled a 'pandemic' by WHO quickly. With no stronger evidence available here, I put this at the minimum allowed probability.

Reference class: October monthly NASA GISTEMP global land-ocean anomalies. By the early 2020s, October values were already near ~1.0°C, and the 2023-2024 global warmth surge likely shifted the 2025 October distribution to around or a bit above the 1.10°C threshold. A cooler 2025 due to internal variability/ENSO mean reversion is plausible, but on outside-view grounds I think October 2025 is more likely to end up at or above 1.10°C than below it.

Base rate: over a 6-month window, the #1 spot on billionaire rankings can change fairly often when the leader’s wealth is tied to volatile equity prices, so an outside-view anchor is around one-third. But conditioning on late-2025 specifics, Musk likely still had a meaningful cushion over the next richest people and a large portion of his wealth was in private assets like SpaceX/xAI, which are less likely to swing enough day-to-day to erase the lead quickly. Since most of the resolution window had already elapsed by Nov. 9, the event was either already triggered or there were only ~7 weeks left for a reversal. My best estimate is that a brief loss of #1 status remained possible via a sharp Tesla drop or rival surge, but was clearly less likely than not.

Moonshot is a credible frontier lab, but by the 2025 landscape the likeliest holders of the top Chatbot Arena slot are still the biggest incumbent labs (especially OpenAI, Google, Anthropic, and xAI). Moonshot’s 2025 momentum makes a surprise jump possible, and Arena rankings can change quickly after a major release, but with only about seven weeks from the cutoff to resolution, Moonshot probably needs a clearly superior new launch rather than just steady improvement. Without evidence that Moonshot is already leading or sitting just behind the leader as of 2025-11-09, I put them in the single-digit range rather than as a co-favorite.

This is a narrow 0.05°C bin for a month that has already finished by the forecast date, so most probability should sit in one or two adjacent brackets rather than be spread widely. My best estimate is that October 2025's NASA GISTEMP anomaly is a bit more likely to come in slightly above this range—around the mid/high 1.1s—reflecting continued exceptional warmth in 2025 but some cooling from the 2024 peak. That makes 1.10–1.14°C plausible, but not my modal outcome.

Base rate first: a specific named person finishing exactly #2 in Google’s global Year in Search 'People' list is a low-probability event, even for a former U.S. president. Biden is more plausible than a random name because he remained globally salient in 2025 and had health-related news that likely produced a meaningful search spike. But the biggest counterpoint is that Google’s Year in Search ranking is about trending versus the prior year, and Biden’s 2024 baseline was already enormous because of the election cycle, debate, and withdrawal. That makes it harder for him to rank this high again in 2025. In addition, 2025 appears to have had several stronger fresh global contenders—especially the papal transition (Pope Francis / Pope Leo XIV), plus Trump and other possible viral figures. So Biden looks live for the top 10, maybe even the top few, but exact #2 still seems only a low-teens outcome.

Crystal Palace are historically a mid-table/top-half outsider rather than a normal title-winning club. In the Premier League era, titles are overwhelmingly concentrated among a tiny set of financial/squad-power elites, with Leicester's 2015-16 season the rare extreme exception. Without access to search tools here, I cannot condition on Palace's actual league position, underlying numbers, injuries, or transfer outcomes as of 2025-11-09, so I have to lean heavily on the outside view. My true unconstrained estimate would be below 5%, but under the tool/instruction floor I submit 5%.

Reference class: Bitcoin touching a downside barrier roughly 45–50% below late-2024 levels within about a year, especially in a post-halving cycle. My outside-view annual base rate for that kind of move is around 15–25%, since BTC is very volatile and has had multiple 40%+ drawdowns, but full 45–50% barrier breaks within a single year are not the modal outcome in post-halving bull periods. By the 2025-11-09 cutoff, most of the window has already elapsed, and my best estimate is that BTC likely had not already traded down to $50k; if it had, it would have required a very conspicuous crash. With only ~52 days left until resolution, a further drop to $50k would still be possible, especially because a 1-minute Binance wick counts, but it would likely require a severe risk-off shock or crypto-specific liquidation cascade. Netting those factors out, I put YES at 9%.

Initial belief state: Reference class = a named AI lab holding the #1 spot on Chatbot Arena at a specific future checkpoint. Base rate: for a pre-specified lab, the chance is modest because leadership is concentrated among a handful of frontier labs and changes quickly; for Mistral specifically, the outside view is below the naive 1/N because it has historically been strong but usually not the best-resourced frontier player versus OpenAI, Google, Anthropic, xAI, Meta, and other major contenders. Key uncertainties are whether Mistral was already near the top as of 2025-11-09, whether it had an imminent flagship release before year-end, and how much Arena-specific optimization might compress the gap. With no search tools available, I lean on the outside view: Mistral is a credible contender but not one of the most likely leaders, and the tie-break rule also slightly hurts it against some alphabetically earlier rivals. Final estimate: 8%.

Reference class: a specific F1 constructor finishing in an exact championship position. In a 10-team field, fifth place is a 10% ex-ante outcome for a random team. Racing Bulls are not a random team, though: they are typically a midfield constructor, so a fifth-place finish is more plausible for them than for the weakest teams, but still not the modal outcome given competition from Williams, Aston Martin, Alpine, Haas, and Sauber for 'best of the rest.' Without a search tool or current standings data as of 2025-11-09, I have to rely on outside-view reasoning rather than late-season points gaps. That pushes me slightly above the 10% exact-position base rate, but not dramatically above it, because Racing Bulls were not an obvious pre-season favorite for P5. So I estimate a low-confidence 14% chance.

Reference class: an individual player winning an invite-heavy, likely single-elimination heads-up poker championship. Outside view says the average entrant’s chance is about 1.5% in a 64-player field or 3% in a 32-player field. Kristen Foxen is an elite tournament player, so she should be above average, but heads-up NLHE is still very high-variance and even top players usually remain in the high-single-digit range unless the field is unusually small or soft. With no reliable pre-cutoff field/bracket information available here, I stay close to that outside view and put her at 6%.

Base rate is low: films opening at Thanksgiving or later rarely finish #1 in domestic calendar-year gross because they have only ~5 weeks of 2025 receipts to count. Zootopia 2 does have a real path — it is a strong Disney animated sequel with a prime Thanksgiving launch and could post excellent holiday legs — but it likely needs a near-best-case run, probably around the high-$300Ms to low-$400Ms domestic by Dec. 31, while also needing the earlier-2025 slate to top out relatively modestly. Given the usual advantage of big spring/summer hits and the fact family animation tends to accumulate a bit more slowly than ultra-frontloaded event movies, I make YES a meaningful but clearly minority outcome.

Initial/base-rate view: this is a question about annual launch cadence for a still-developmental heavy-lift rocket system. Starship’s flight rate had been rising year over year, but not remotely to a sustained 10–12 successful-to-space launches/year pace by early November 2025. Using public facts known by the cutoff, 2025 appears to have had only a few Starship launches that reached space by 2025-11-09 (roughly flights 7–9, i.e. about 3). To resolve YES, SpaceX would then need about 7–9 additional successful-to-space launches in the final ~7 weeks of the year. That is far beyond demonstrated Starship cadence, especially given the program’s test-mishap/regulatory/vehicle-readiness bottlenecks. Because the outcome is not literally impossible, I stay at the floor 5%, but the true odds seem lower than that.

Base rate is 20% for a five-team division, then adjusted upward because Indiana entered this window as a legitimate upper-tier Eastern team built around Haliburton and Siakam, with more growth upside than Chicago or Detroit. But Cleveland and Milwaukee both plausibly project as equal or stronger regular-season teams, so Indiana should be seen as a contender, not the favorite. With no external search tools available here and substantial uncertainty about 2025 offseason moves, injuries, and early-season standings as of 2025-11-09, I land a bit above the equal-share base rate.

Outside-view, a U.S. invasion of Iranian territory is an extreme-tail event. The resolution standard is also narrow: it requires a military offensive intended to establish control over some portion of Iran, not just airstrikes, naval clashes, or covert action. For a large, capable state like Iran, the costs, logistics, and escalation risks are enormous, and U.S. behavior in past Iran crises has usually stopped short of territorial occupation. By a November 2025 forecast date, the remaining time in the year is short, which further suppresses the chance. I therefore put this at the floor-ish end of the range.

Reference class: years in which the US is militarily active around Yemen but has no declared conventional ground campaign. In that class, publicly reported entry of regular non-SOF US troops onto Yemeni soil is rare. My inside-view adjustment is that 2025 featured unusually intense US strikes on the Houthis, which creates some chance of an emergency landing, rescue/recovery, or limited conventional visit. But the campaign was still overwhelmingly air/naval, the most plausible clandestine ground activity (SOF/advisers) is excluded, and by the Nov. 9 forecast date most of the eligible window had already passed without any highly salient consensus reporting of regular troops entering Yemen. That leaves only a small residual probability for a late-2025 incident or for an obscure-but-credible report to surface by year-end.

Reference class: a named player winning a single-elimination heads-up poker championship with roughly 32-64 entrants. An equal-skill baseline would be about 1.6%-3.1%. Marius Gierse is plausibly better than average in heads-up NLHE, so he deserves a material uplift over baseline, but poker heads-up brackets are still very high-variance and require winning 5-6 straight matches. Without firmer information on the exact field, format, and his confirmed participation status as of 2025-11-09, a low-single-digit to high-single-digit chance is most reasonable. I land at 6%.

Reference class: which frontier lab owns the #1 Chatbot Arena model at a year-end snapshot. Outside view, Google is one of roughly 3-4 serious contenders, giving it a base chance in the mid-20s. I push that up slightly because Google has elite model-development capacity, strong odds of another Gemini refresh before year-end, and the alphabetical tiebreak helps it against some rivals like OpenAI/xAI (though not Anthropic). I keep it well below 50% because arena leadership is volatile and OpenAI/Anthropic have historically been at least as strong in chat-preference leaderboards. Net: Google is a major contender, but not the single most likely owner of the top spot.

Base rate for formal WHO pandemic declarations is very low—roughly only a couple of clear cases in recent decades—and by the 2025-11-09 cutoff no widely recognized WHO pandemic declaration had occurred in 2025. With only about seven weeks left in the year, YES would most likely require a fast-moving deterioration in an existing outbreak such as H5N1 or mpox severe enough for WHO to use the specific word 'pandemic,' a label it has used sparingly. That keeps the remaining hazard nonzero but low.

Initial belief state and final forecast in one step, since no external search tool is available. Reference class: incumbent heads of government in stable parliamentary democracies, especially German chancellors, over a very short remaining horizon (about 7 weeks from the 2025-11-09 cutoff to 2025-12-31). Base rate for an unexpected exit over such a short window is very low—roughly low single digits—because German chancellors are institutionally hard to remove and usually remain in office unless there is a major scandal, coalition rupture with resignation, incapacity, or a successful constructive vote of no confidence. I adjust slightly upward from that base rate because Merz is a relatively new chancellor and new coalition governments can be more fragile than long-settled incumbencies. Still, with no scheduled leadership transition before year-end and little time remaining, the most likely outcome is that he stays in office through Dec. 31. Final estimate: 6%.

Initial belief state: Reference class is near-threshold nuclear states potentially crossing to an actual, credibly confirmed bomb within a fixed short horizon. Base rate is low: true nuclear proliferation events are rare, and confirmed first possession by a specific deadline is rarer still. Iran is a special case because it has long had the technical base and enough enriched material to make breakout faster than most states, which raises the hazard above the generic base rate. Still, as of a Nov. 9, 2025 forecast for a Dec. 31, 2025 resolution, the remaining window is short, and the market requires credible confirmation, not just latent capability. My estimate is therefore 11%, with low confidence because I do not have search tools here to verify late-2025 developments.

I’d put Meta at 14%. Outside view: by late 2025 there are several credible contenders for the top Chatbot Arena slot—most notably OpenAI, Google, Anthropic, xAI, and Meta, with a few dark horses. A generic major lab might have something like a mid-teens chance, but I discount Meta somewhat because its models have historically been very competitive without being the most frequent absolute Arena leader, and the alphabetical tie-break is mildly unfavorable versus several key rivals. The forecast horizon from 2025-11-09 to 2025-12-31 is short, so unless Meta is already near the top or has an imminent flagship release, there is limited time to overtake the field. My biggest uncertainty is the actual leaderboard state and release pipeline as of the cutoff date; lacking that direct information, I keep confidence low and stay below 20%.

Base rate for any NFL team is 1/32 (3.1%), but Washington should be above that as of 2025-11-09 because they were coming off a major 2024 breakout with Jayden Daniels and had added notable supporting talent. Still, Super Bowl paths are brutal: single-elimination variance is high, and the Commanders would have to get through a crowded NFC and then beat an AFC champion likely as strong or stronger. Without a live search tool to verify their exact midseason record/injuries/seeding, I land in the upper-single-digit range rather than treating them as a top favorite.

Outside view dominates here. A 14-week consecutive run at #1 on the Billboard 200 is an exceptionally rare blockbuster outcome even for albums that debut at #1, and this contract also resolves NO if the album fails to debut at #1 or is not released in time. Without reliable album-specific chart information up to the 2025-11-09 cutoff in this environment, the best calibrated estimate is near the floor: only a small minority of even highly anticipated albums clear this bar.

I’d put this at slightly better than even odds. Outside view: 2025 is the first full post-halving year, historically the window when Bitcoin often makes its cycle high, and $140k is roughly around a ‘central’ next-cycle peak estimate (about ~2x the prior ATH zone rather than an extreme moonshot). The market only needs one Binance 1-minute candle high, which modestly boosts YES odds versus requiring a sustained trade. Offsetting that, by the 2025-11-09 cutoff there are only about 7 weeks left if the threshold has not already been hit, so the estimate is very sensitive to the actual BTC level and momentum at that date. Without search/data access to verify the contemporaneous price path, I lean slight YES but with low confidence.

Initial/base-rate forecast only. The relevant reference class is a named top offensive rookie prospect—especially a likely early-drafted WR—winning NFL Offensive Rookie of the Year. For any single player in that class, the prior is usually in the mid-single digits to low teens: the award is won by only one player, QBs often have an edge, and WRs need both strong production and no dominant rookie QB season. Luther Burden was widely viewed as a strong WR prospect, which keeps him above a generic rookie, but absent contemporaneous mid-2025 season evidence on draft slot, team situation, health, and production through 2025-11-09, I should stay conservative. I therefore put him at 8%.

Base rate first: by early November, a plausible Best Picture contender usually sits in a crowded field of roughly 10–15 live candidates, so any one film starts in the high-single digits unless it is a clear runaway frontrunner. For Sentimental Value, the upside is that Joachim Trier is a respected auteur with prior Oscar recognition, and this kind of acclaimed festival/crossover title can absolutely break into Best Picture. The downside is that an art-house/international-leaning drama typically has a harder path from 'admired' to actually winning the preferential-ballot top prize, and it still must first secure a nomination. With no stronger late-cutoff evidence available here, I put it as a real but secondary contender rather than the field leader.

Base rate first: the yearly top domestic opener is usually one of a small handful of mega-tentpoles, so any single named film generally starts in the low-to-mid teens unless it is the clear consensus frontrunner. 'Wicked: For Good' has strong brand/IP, a sequel boost, and a favorable holiday-season frame, so it deserves to be above a generic contender. But against the 2025 slate known by mid-2024, it still looks less like a lock than more traditional top-opener candidates such as 'Avatar 3', a 'Jurassic World' film, 'Superman', or 'Fantastic Four'. Also, musicals and holiday releases can be less frontloaded than the kinds of franchise films that often win annual opening-weekend crowns. Since I do not have search capability here, I cannot update on the crucial late-breaking information that would have been available by 2025-11-09 (actual 2025 openings to date, pre-release tracking, and the performance of the first 'Wicked'). So I’m leaning on outside-view reasoning and putting it at 18%.

Initial outside-view forecast only, since no search tools are available in this environment. Best Production Design usually goes to visually dominant period pieces, fantasies, musicals, or large-scale epics. A Springsteen/Nebraska biopic has some plausible upside from 1980s period recreation and awards-friendly prestige packaging, but it sounds more like an acting/screenplay vehicle than an obvious production-design showcase. It also still faces release-timing, nomination, and competition risks. Net: very low chance, at the floor of 5%.

Reference class: an individual player’s chance to win an invitational single-elimination heads-up poker championship, likely with a large bracket (historically often 32–64 players). A neutral entrant in a 64-player field would be about 1.6%; in a 32-player field about 3.1%. Ryan Feldman is best known as a poker media/operator figure rather than as an elite heads-up specialist, so absent evidence that the field is unusually soft or that he had already advanced deep by the 2025-11-09 cutoff, his true win chance looks below the average serious pro’s. I therefore set a very low but nonzero probability at 2%.

DeepSeek is a real frontier contender, but as of the 2025-11-09 cutoff I would still expect the Chatbot Arena year-end leader to come more often from OpenAI, Google, Anthropic, or xAI. The outside-view base rate for any single lab among the handful of plausible leaders is only modest, and the inside-view adjustment is negative because only about seven weeks remain for DeepSeek to launch a model that both reaches #1 and is still on top at the exact Dec. 31 checkpoint. DeepSeek’s demonstrated ability to surprise keeps this well above zero, but it is still a clear underdog.

Base rate for 'which lab holds the #1 Chatbot Arena slot at a near-term checkpoint' suggests OpenAI is one of a small handful of plausible leaders, but not an outright majority favorite. Over the 2023-2025 frontier-model era, the top spot has rotated among OpenAI, Google, Anthropic, xAI and occasionally others; a reasonable outside-view anchor is roughly one-third for OpenAI. I then adjust slightly upward because Arena historically rewards polished chat preference optimization, an area where OpenAI is especially strong, and because a 7-week horizon leaves room for incremental model updates. I adjust downward for fierce competition from Google/Anthropic/xAI and because OpenAI loses many possible ties on alphabetical order. Net: OpenAI looks like a strong contender but still more likely than not to be overtaken or tied unfavorably by year-end.

Base rate first: official US government declarations that a newly discovered astronomical object is extraterrestrial life/technology are extraordinarily rare—effectively zero in the modern era. This market is even narrower, requiring a definitive statement by very senior officials or a federal agency by a near-term deadline. Even if A11pl3Z is unusual, the evidentiary bar for the US government to say it is alien life/technology is extremely high, and bureaucratic/scientific caution strongly favors delay or ambiguity over a definitive claim. Without concrete pre-cutoff evidence here that the object showed unmistakably artificial or biological signatures, the outside view dominates. I therefore put this at the floor-allowed 5%.

Reference class: exact-path Fed policy questions, specifically whether a calendar year ends with exactly four 25bp-equivalent cuts. Ex ante, that is a fairly narrow outcome and probably around a low-teens base rate. By the Nov. 9, 2025 cutoff, only the Dec. 9-10 FOMC meeting remained. That means YES was only realistic if the Fed had already delivered something like 3 cuts (then a 25bp December cut would do it) or 4 cuts (then a December hold would preserve it). If cumulative 2025 cuts were only 0-2 by then, reaching exactly 4 in one remaining meeting would require an unusually large 50-100bp move, which is rare; if 5+ had already happened, the market was already dead. Given the 2025 macro backdrop implied by a cautious Fed—sticky inflation risk, tariff/political uncertainty, and no obvious reason to expect an outsized December move—the most likely states by early November were below the needed path. So I put YES at only 7%.

Base rate for any one NBA team to win the NBA Cup is about 1/30, or 3.3%. Portland entered this era as a rebuilding, non-contending team, and even allowing for the higher variance of a short group-stage plus knockout tournament, they would usually project below average rather than among the leading favorites. Because I do not have search tools here to verify Portland’s exact 2025-11-09 roster, form, injuries, or cup path, I’m relying on outside-view reasoning and staying at the minimum allowed probability. My estimate is that Portland had about a 5% chance to win the 2025 NBA Cup as of 2025-11-09.

This is an extreme-tail event. The relevant reference class—non-nuclear US allies newly acquiring independent operational nuclear weapons in a single year—has an exceptionally low historical base rate. By the Nov. 9, 2025 cutoff, there was substantial proliferation rhetoric among some US allies, but no widely established indication that any eligible ally had already crossed the line into possessing an independently controlled operational nuclear weapon. With only about seven weeks left until resolution, the remaining path to YES would most plausibly require either a covert program already essentially complete or a sudden revelation of an existing weapon, both low-probability scenarios. I therefore put this at the floor-consistent low probability of 5%.

I estimate a 14% chance. The core reference class is direct kinetic incidents between NATO and Russian forces during periods of extreme tension/proxy conflict. Historically these are rare, and even during the intense Russia-Ukraine war the dominant pattern has been brinkmanship without direct exchange of fire. That said, the risk is not negligible: the war is adjacent to NATO territory, air and naval encounters are recurrent, and this market’s criteria are broad enough that a shootdown of a non-munition UAV could qualify. Overall, mutual deterrence and the narrow exclusions in the rules keep NO as the clear favorite, but a low-teens YES probability is warranted.

I estimate an 18% chance. My outside-view anchor is that successor releases for first-generation Apple hardware platforms within roughly two years are not the norm, and Vision Pro looks more like a niche, expensive platform with a slower cadence than an annual iPhone-style line. The inside view also leans No: weak urgency to refresh a costly mixed-reality device, likely lower sales than core Apple products, and a very short remaining window to both announce and make a successor generally available by Dec. 31, 2025. The main path to Yes is a relatively quiet iterative refresh—possibly a chip update or renamed second-generation Vision device—that clearly counts as the successor under the market’s broad resolution criteria. But absent strong evidence of an imminent launch, No remains the more likely outcome.

Base rate for any specific NFL team to win the upcoming Super Bowl is about 1 in 32 (~3%). By early November, a plausible contender with a strong franchise/QB core is typically in the high-single-digits rather than 3%, but even elite teams rarely get above the mid-teens because the path still requires making the playoffs, winning multiple single-elimination games, and then the Super Bowl. The Eagles project as a credible contender rather than the field favorite, so I land at 9%, with low confidence because I do not have team-specific November 2025 search evidence available in this environment.

I estimate about a 6% chance of a qualifying divorce-intention announcement by Dec. 31, 2025, using base rates and publicly known background rather than fresh reporting. The key driver is timing: as of the Nov. 9, 2025 knowledge cutoff, only about seven weeks remained in the resolution window. For a couple married since 2005, with very large political and reputational incentives to avoid a public split—especially while Trump is in office—the hazard of a formal public divorce announcement over that short remaining period seems low. Against that, Trump has divorced before and the marriage has long attracted speculation, so the risk is not zero. But rumors are not enough for resolution; there needs to be an announcement by them/their reps or overwhelming credible-media consensus. Overall that points to a low-single-digit to high-single-digit probability, and I land at 6%.

My estimate is low. By the Nov. 9, 2025 cutoff, Musk appears to have already left his formal early-2025 government role, and the most salient public development afterward was a sharp break with Trump rather than a visible path back into office.
